data.title: As a test-writing agent, I consume a shared behavioral testing standard so my tests resist refactoring
data.description:
## Description

**What**: Create a shared behavioral testing standard as a prompt fragment at plugins/dso/skills/shared/prompts/behavioral-testing-standard.md, consumable by all test-writing agent prompts.
**Why**: Testing guidance is currently duplicated and inconsistent across RED test writer, fix-bug, sprint task execution, and review resolution. A single source of truth ensures all agents follow the same 4-rule decision tree.
**Scope**:
- IN: The shared prompt fragment with 4 sequential rules: (1) before writing — check for existing coverage, (2) what to test — Given/When/Then, one test one behavior, no internal method names, (3) how to test — execute code, assert observable outcomes, never read source files in assertions, mock only external boundaries, (4) after writing — refactoring litmus test
- IN: Grounded in Google's unchanging test principle, Khorikov's resistance-to-refactoring pillar, and Sam Hatoum's refactoring litmus test
- OUT: Modifying any consumer agent prompts (those are separate stories)
- OUT: Modifying implementation-plan task output format

## Done Definitions

- When this story is complete, a prompt fragment exists at plugins/dso/skills/shared/prompts/behavioral-testing-standard.md containing 4 sequential rules that can be referenced by any agent
  ← Satisfies: "All test-writing agent prompts consume a shared behavioral testing standard with 4 sequential rules"
- When this story is complete, unit tests written and passing for all new or modified logic
  ← Satisfies: TDD done-of-done

## Considerations

- [Maintainability] Shared prompt fragment consumed by 5+ agents — single-source-of-truth pattern; each consumer must reference, not duplicate

## Escalation Policy

**Escalation policy**: Proceed unless a significant assumption is required to continue — one that could send the implementation in the wrong direction. Escalate only when genuinely blocked without a reasonable inference. Document all assumptions made without escalating.
